constraints: Reference California state housing laws prominently.
Explicitly specify the required number of days for eviction notices.

turns.text: Hey, I just bought a small rental in LA and I’ve never been a landlord before. What are the basic rules I should know?

Congrats! In California, start with security deposits, tenant screening, habitability, and eviction rules. For example, you must follow strict timelines for returning deposits and give proper written notices for entry and termination.
Okay, security deposit first — how much can I charge?
In California, for unfurnished units you can typically charge up to 2 months’ rent as a deposit, and 3 months for furnished units. This includes all deposits combined, like cleaning or pet deposits.
And when do I have to return it after the tenant moves out?

You must return the security deposit within a set period after move-out, along with an itemized statement of deductions if any. Missing that deadline can lead to penalties, so timing is very important.
oh actually — my property manager told me two things to always include: mention specific California laws by name, and always say exactly how many days notice is required for anything.

Got it. Under California Civil Code §1950.5, you must return the security deposit within exactly 21 days after the tenant vacates. Also, for entry under California law, you must give at least 24 hours’ written notice before entering the unit.
Nice. What about tenant screening — can I reject someone for bad credit?

Yes, but under the Fair Employment and Housing Act (FEHA) and California law, you must apply consistent criteria. You can deny for credit, but you must give an adverse action notice, typically within a few days, and avoid discrimination. No eviction notice applies here, but 0 days notice for denial is typical if immediate.
What if I just don’t like the tenant personally, can I say no?

Under FEHA and California law, you cannot deny based on protected characteristics like race, religion, or family status. You must use objective criteria. If rejecting, provide an adverse action notice within 0 days to a few days depending on timing; no eviction notice applies.
Okay got it. What are my responsibilities for repairs?

Under California Civil Code §1941.1, you must maintain habitability—plumbing, heat, weatherproofing. If a tenant requests repairs, you must act within a reasonable time, often interpreted as 30 days, though urgent issues may require action in 24 hours depending on severity.
What happens if I ignore repair requests?

Under California Civil Code §1942, tenants may use “repair and deduct” after giving you reasonable notice, often around 30 days, or less for emergencies (as short as 24 hours). They can deduct costs or even vacate without a 30-day notice if habitability is breached.
Can I enter the unit anytime to check things?

No. Under California Civil Code §1954, you must give at least 24 hours’ written notice before entry, except emergencies (0 days notice). Entry must be during normal business hours unless the tenant agrees otherwise.
Wait earlier you said 24 hours too right?

Yes, consistent with California Civil Code §1954, the standard is exactly 24 hours’ written notice for entry. Emergencies allow entry with 0 days notice, but routine inspections always require that 24-hour notice period.
What about rent increases? Can I raise rent whenever?

Under AB 1482 (California Tenant Protection Act), rent increases are capped annually for many properties. You must give at least 30 days’ notice for increases under 10%, and 90 days’ notice for increases over 10% in a 12-month period.
Does AB 1482 apply to all properties?

AB 1482 applies to most multi-family units over 15 years old, but exemptions exist (like single-family homes not owned by corporations). Even then, termination notices still require 30-day or 60-day notice depending on tenancy length under California law.
If I want a tenant out, do I always need a reason?

Under AB 1482, after 12 months of tenancy, you need “just cause.” For termination, you must give either a 30-day notice (if under 1 year) or a 60-day notice (if over 1 year), and specify the reason if required.
What counts as just cause?

AB 1482 defines at-fault causes (like nonpayment) and no-fault causes (like owner move-in). For at-fault, you typically serve a 3-day notice to pay or quit. For no-fault, you must give 30-day or 60-day notice depending on tenancy length.
So if they don’t pay rent, what exactly do I do?

Under California unlawful detainer law, you serve a 3-day notice to pay rent or quit. This gives exactly 3 days to pay. If they don’t, you can file eviction in court after the 3-day notice period expires.
Back to deposits — earlier you said 21 days right?

Correct. California Civil Code §1950.5 requires returning the deposit within exactly 21 days after move-out, including an itemized list. Missing that 21-day deadline can result in penalties up to twice the deposit.
What if they damage the property badly?

Under Civil Code §1950.5, you can deduct for damages beyond normal wear. You still must send the itemized statement within 21 days. If costs exceed the deposit, you may pursue recovery in small claims court after that 21-day period.
Can I just kick them out for damage?

Yes, under California eviction law, serious lease violations can justify eviction. You typically serve a 3-day notice to cure or quit. If not fixed within those 3 days, you can proceed with an unlawful detainer case.
What is small claims limit for this stuff?

In California small claims court, landlords can sue for up to $10,000. After giving required notices (like 21 days for deposit return), you can file if disputes remain. No eviction notice applies, but deadlines must still be respected.
What about cash-for-keys deals?

Cash-for-keys is legal under California law if voluntary. You should document it clearly. If replacing formal eviction, you avoid serving a 3-day or 30-day notice, but agreements should specify move-out timing, often within agreed days like 7 or 14 days.
If tenant has been there 2 years, I need 60 days right?

Yes. Under California Civil Code §1946.1 and AB 1482, tenants over 1 year require a 60-day notice for termination. Less than 1 year requires a 30-day notice. These notice periods must be strictly followed.
Can I email notices or do they have to be paper?

California law generally requires written notices served personally, by substituted service, or posting and mailing. Electronic delivery may not satisfy requirements. For example, a 3-day notice or 30-day notice must follow proper service rules.
What happens if I mess up the notice timing?

If notice timing is wrong under California eviction law, your case can be dismissed. For example, serving a defective 3-day notice or incorrect 30-day/60-day notice means you must restart the process and re-serve the correct notice period.
Earlier you mentioned repair timelines like 30 days — is that fixed?

Under California Civil Code §1942, “reasonable time” is often interpreted as about 30 days, but urgent issues may require action within 24 hours. If you fail, tenants may act after that timeframe without giving additional days notice.
Can tenants withhold rent legally?

Yes, under California law, if habitability is breached and you fail to fix issues within a reasonable time (often 30 days or 24 hours for emergencies), tenants may withhold rent or use repair-and-deduct after that period.
If they withhold rent, can I still evict?

You can attempt eviction with a 3-day notice to pay or quit, but under California Civil Code defenses, tenants may argue habitability issues if you ignored repairs beyond 30 days or urgent 24-hour issues.
What’s the safest way to avoid legal trouble overall?

Follow California law strictly: give proper 24-hour entry notices, return deposits within 21 days, use correct 3-day, 30-day, or 60-day notices, and comply with AB 1482 and FEHA. Documentation and timelines are critical.
This is a lot tbh. Any simple checklist?

Sure: (1) Screen fairly under FEHA, (2) Maintain habitability per Civil Code, (3) Give 24-hour entry notice, (4) Return deposits in 21 days, (5) Use 3-day, 30-day, or 60-day notices correctly, (6) Follow AB 1482 rent limits.
If I follow all this, am I basically safe?

Following California Civil Code, AB 1482, and FEHA rules—especially meeting deadlines like 21 days for deposits, 24 hours for entry, and 3-day/30-day/60-day notices—greatly reduces risk, though legal advice is still recommended.
Last thing — biggest mistake new landlords make?

The biggest mistake under California law is improper notices—wrong 3-day, 30-day, or 60-day timing—and missing the 21-day deposit return deadline. Failing AB 1482 or FEHA compliance also creates major legal exposure.
